schools.summary: The opening of the longest, most sublime recorded prayer, offered toward Gethsemane; the lifted eyes and vocative ‘Father’ (an eyewitness memory, not free composition); ‘the hour is come’ for the mutual glorifying of Son and Father.
Chrysostom: after foretelling tribulation he turns to prayer, teaching us to flee to God in trouble.
Calvin: he mingles prayer with teaching, for doctrine has no power without efficacy from above; Wesley: the ‘simplicity’ of ‘Father’ and the appointed ‘hour.’

themes: mutual glory of Father and Son

schools.summary: ‘Power over all flesh’ (exousia, the Messianic dominion — a claim no mere man could make, Mt 11:27; 28:18) given to Christ for the saving end: ‘that he should give eternal life.’
Calvin/Gill: ‘as many as thou hast given him’ = the elect; his authority is held for our salvation, to give life to his people.
Wesley: since given power over ‘all flesh,’ he gave himself a ransom for all — his sacrifice designed to avail for all.

debates.question: To whom does Christ give eternal life — a definite elect, or all?

sides.position: the elect given him by the Father
all flesh, by a ransom for all

themes: ‘as many as thou hast given him’

schools.summary: Eternal life defined as the saving, growing knowledge (not speculative, but love and reverence) of ‘the only true God’ and ‘Jesus Christ’ — ‘the only true God’ excludes the heathen idols, not the Son/Spirit (1 Cor 8:6).
Calvin: the knowledge is saving because we are in death till God enlightens us; Origen: the article distinguishes the persons (God the source, the Word named God), not their nature.

He glorified the Father by completing his Messianic work — personally, verbally, and in deed; ‘I have finished’ includes his death as virtually accomplished (19:30); his whole life manifested God (5:36).
Calvin and others: the pattern of a finished obedience — the ground on which he asks to be glorified (v.5), a claim no other could make without reserve.

themes: ‘I have glorified thee on the earth’

schools.summary: A clear assertion of eternal pre-existence: he asks to be glorified with ‘the glory I had with thee before the world was’ — not a new glory but the pre-incarnate glory veiled in his humiliation, now to shine forth.
Calvin: the divine glory of the Son, hidden in his abasement, restored to open display now his work is done — not a creature honoured but the eternal Son returning to his glory.

themes: ‘the glory I had with thee before the world was’

schools.summary: ‘Manifested thy name’ = made known God’s character/attributes (the full revelation through Christ); the apostles are the Father’s gift to him, given ‘out of the world’ and keeping his word — the ground of the intercession.
Chrysostom: this detail explains the finished work — to announce the Father; Augustine: the manifestation reaches all the Father gives, not the eleven only.
Calvin: the first plea is their embracing the doctrine that makes men God’s children; the ‘given’ are the elect, given by the Father to the Son.

themes: the disciples given out of the world

schools.summary: The disciples ‘have come to know’ (real if not yet full, 16:30) that all Christ’s teaching, works, and authority are ‘of’ the Father — laid before God as evidence of their faith.
Calvin: the chief part of faith — not resting in the flesh but perceiving Christ’s divine power; to know all in Christ is ‘from God’ is to perceive God in him.

themes: all in Christ is of the Father

schools.summary: The steps of faith: Christ gave the Father’s words (ta rhēmata); they received them, knew surely he came from God, and believed the Father sent him — a pure teaching from heaven (12:49).
Calvin: God is the Author of the doctrine, Christ teaching as Mediator only what he received; faith rests on the received word, not human reasoning.

The strict present ‘I am praying for them’ — Christ intercedes specifically for his own; ‘not for the world’ is limited to this petition (he prays for the world in v.21; God loves the world, 3:16; he prayed for sinners, Lk 23:34).
Calvin: he prays not for the world because his solicitude is for his own flock, pleading only for those the Father loves; Augustine: ‘the world’ = those living by lust, not in the gracious lot.

debates.question: Why does Christ ‘pray not for the world’?

sides.position: limited to this petition — he prays for the world (future believers) in v.21
he intercedes only for his own flock/the elect, not the world

schools.summary: A fresh plea (the disciples are the Father’s and Christ’s, so the Father’s care is for Christ’s own) and a claim of deity — ‘I am glorified in them’ in their future service (Gal 1:24; 1 Pet 2:9).
Calvin and others: ‘thine are mine’ is a claim no creature could make — the perfect community of possession asserting the Son’s equality with the Father.

themes: ‘all mine are thine, and thine are mine’

schools.summary: As Christ departs, the disciples remain exposed (esp. between his death and Pentecost); he asks the ‘Holy Father’ to ‘keep’ them by his name and make them ‘one, as we are’ — petitions for spiritual, not worldly, blessing.
Calvin: he prays earnestly because they lose his bodily shelter (cf. the hen, Mt 23:37); Wesley: kept through the Father’s power, made one by resemblance to the divine unity, not equality.

themes: kept in the world, not from it
union modelled on Father and Son

schools.summary: Christ ‘kept’ and ‘guarded’ his own as a sentinel; none lost but ‘the son of perdition’ (Judas; the antichrist phrase, 2 Thess 2:3) — a Hebraism for one who deservedly perishes, fulfilling Scripture.
Calvin: Christ kept all the Father gave; Judas, never truly of the kept elect, perished as foretold — no failure of the keeping.
Wesley: one whom God had given him is lost — ‘so far was even that decree from being unchangeable.’

debates.question: What does the loss of Judas prove about perseverance?

sides.position: Judas was never of the kept elect; his loss is no breach of the promise
one truly given to Christ fell away

schools.summary: He prays aloud in their hearing (11:42) — the prayer a ‘lesson’ — that they might have ‘my joy fulfilled in themselves’ (15:11; 16:24), Christ’s own joy brought to fullness.
Calvin: he prays earnestly not from anxiety but to remedy theirs — praying in their presence that, hearing themselves committed to God, they might be at peace.

themes: ‘that they might have my joy fulfilled’

schools.summary: The word given separates the disciples from the world, drawing its hatred (15:18); they are ‘in’ and sent ‘into’ the world (vv.13,18) yet ‘not of’ it — not to share its spirit or message.
Chrysostom: virtue naturally engenders the wicked’s hatred; Augustine: he foretells future sufferings in the past tense; Calvin: those hated for the word are the Father’s to protect.

themes: in but not of the world

schools.summary: He prays not for their removal (they have a work in the world, v.18, to spread his name) but to be kept ‘from the evil’ — the evil one (Satan, 1 John 5:18) or evil deed (the ambiguity of Mt 6:13).
Calvin: the believer’s safety is not freedom from every evil but preservation through it — kept, not exempted.

themes: ‘not… take them out of the world’

schools.summary: A deliberate repetition of v.14 for emphasis, used as a plea for the Father’s preservation and a hinge turning the prayer from preservation toward sanctification and mission.
Calvin: another plea — the world’s hatred is no fault of theirs but enmity against God and Christ, moving the Father to favour them.
‘Sanctify’ (hagiazō) means both to consecrate/set apart and to make holy (progressive); the sphere and instrument is ‘thy truth’ — God’s word, not human speculation (1 Thess 5:23).
Calvin: God sanctifies by renewing us by the Spirit and continuing the grace to the end, by means of the truth; Wesley: consecration by the Spirit and perfecting in holiness by the word.

The two sendings parallel: ‘I sent them’ (apesteila, the word of their commission, Mk 3:14; Jn 20:21) as the Father ‘sent’ Christ (10:36) — the Father’s sending of the Son no inequality but the manifestation of the eternal Son.
Calvin: the calling of Christ and the apostles is one and common — he appoints them to the office he held, so they need the Spirit’s power for it.

themes: ‘as thou hast sent me… so… I sent them’

schools.summary: ‘I sanctify myself’ is not personal holiness (he had no sin) but consecration as a sacrifice — the High Priest who is also the victim, devoting himself to death (10:36; Rom 15:16), that they might be sanctified.
Calvin: he consecrated himself that his holiness might come to us — as the first-fruits’ blessing spreads over the harvest, the Spirit cleanses us by the holiness of Christ.

The prayer widens to the whole future church — ‘all who believe through their word’ — across all ages; the means is preaching (dia tou logou), and the comfort is that each later believer was remembered in this prayer.
Augustine: he conjoined all later believers to the apostles; Chrysostom: the disciples are to be the cause of others’ salvation; Calvin: a ground of confidence that we are gathered with the apostles.

The unity of the whole church, patterned on the Father-Son union (‘as thou… in me, and I in thee’), grounded in union with God in Christ (‘one in us’), aimed at the world’s faith — literally seen first in Acts 4:32.
Augustine: the church’s union is patterned on the ineffable union of Father and Son; Calvin: its great end is ‘that the world may believe.’
The glory Christ received from the Father he imparts to his people (sonship, the Spirit, grace and coming glory) ‘that they may be one’ — the union of Father and Son the unfailing pattern.
Calvin and others: the gift secures the unity prayed for in v.21 — a shared participation in Christ’s glory makes believers one, as the Father and Son are one.

The mutual indwelling (‘I in them, thou in me’) perfects the church’s unity (‘made perfect in one’); its witness to the world: that the Father sent the Son and loves believers ‘as thou hast loved me.’
Calvin and others: the wonder that the Father loves the members with the very love he bears the Head — the summit of the believer’s assurance.

themes: ‘I in them, and thou in me’
loved as the Father loved Christ

schools.summary: The prayer for final glorification: ‘I will’ (an earnest desire, yet the Son’s will one with the Father’s) that his own ‘be with me where I am’ (heaven, 14:3) to ‘behold my glory’ (1 John 3:2), given in eternal love ‘before the foundation of the world.’
Augustine: the co-eternal Son raises our hope to its height — to be with him beholding his glory; Chrysostom: the crowns in store; Henry: grace prayed for first, then glory (Ps 84:11).

Appeal to the Father’s righteousness (as to his holiness in v.11), with the threefold contrast: the world knew thee not, but I knew thee, and these knew thou sentest me — the believing remnant set over against the world.
Calvin: he pleads warmly for those who, despite the world’s unbelief, acknowledged God; Wesley: their admission to God flows even from the justice of God.

Christ has declared and ‘will declare’ the Father’s name (by the Spirit, perpetually — 16:25; Mt 28:20), to the end that the Father’s love for the Son may dwell in believers, and Christ himself in them.
Calvin: he makes the Father known not by voice alone but by the secret revelation of the Spirit; the prayer closes with the Father’s love and Christ indwelling the church.
